subject: English Language

question:
Choose the option that has the same consonant sound as the one represented by the letter(s) underlined.

King
solution:
King - /kɪŋ/

Quite - /kwaɪt/

Knee - /ni:/

Sing - /sɪŋ/

Knight - /naɪt/
subject: English Language

question:
Choose the option that rhymes with the given word. 

moth
solution:
moth - /mɒθ/

both - /bəʊθ/

what - /wɒt/

wrath - /rɒθ/

boat - /bəʊt/
subject: English Language

question:
Choose the appropriate stress from the options. The stressed syllable is written in capital letters.

dedication
solution: For polysyllabic words that end with -ion, the stress tends to be placed on the penultimate syllable.
subject: English Language

question:
The word in capital letters has the emphatic stress. Choose the option to which the given stress relates. 

We WALKED to the school.
solution: Emphatic stress is the placement of greater stress on a particular word in an utterance. This is usually done for clarification or emphasis. The emphasis on "WALKED" in the sentence suggests that the focus is on the action of walking, implying that it is being contrasted with another action, such as running. Therefore, the question "Did we run to the school?" is a logical counterpart, as it contrasts running with walking.

options.a: Did we walk to the stadium?
options.b: Did we run to the school?
options.c: Did they walk to the school?
options.d: Did we walk to the church?

subject: English Language

question:
The word in capital letters has the emphatic stress. Choose the option to which the given stress relates. 

My mother is expected to be back by this time TOMORROW.
solution: Emphatic stress is the placement of greater stress on a particular word in an utterance. This is done for clarification or emphasis. The emphasis on "TOMORROW" in the sentence highlights the specific time frame being discussed. The contrast implied by the emphasis is between "tomorrow" and another time frame, such as "next month." This makes option B the most logical choice, as it contrasts "tomorrow" with "next month."

options.a: Who is expected back by this time tomorrow?
options.b: Is my mother expected back by this time next month?
options.c: Is my mother assumed back by this time tomorrow?
options.d: Is my mother expected gone by this time tomorrow?

subject: English Language

question: Select the option that fills the gap labelled 6
solution: The sentence discusses how monolingual standard languages are seen as the norm and anything differing from this is considered aberrant. "Deviations" fits as it means variations or departures from the standard.
section:
The passage below has gaps. Following each gap, four options are provided. Choose the most appropriate option for each gap.

One way in which we come to see monolingual standard languages as the norm and __6__ [A. deviations B. aberrations C. criticisms D. deviants] from the imagined monolingual standard language as aberrant, and remember each individual __7__ [A. repertoire B. word C. idea D. statement] is a deviation but repertoires characterised by greater deviation as problematic is through the close association between language and place. You would have seen maps that map languages into territory. You probably can __8__ [A. write B. set C. conjure D. let] in your mind a map of the Americas where almost all of North America is __9__ [A. known B.  coded C. accepted D. conceded] for English except for a bit of French in East Canada, more than half of Central and South America would be coded for Spanish, the other big chunk (Brazil) for Portuguese, and three tiny __10__ [A. havens B. colonies C. isles D. pockets] of English, Dutch and French where the states of Guyana, Suriname and French Guiana are located in addition to maps based on the __11__ [A. national B. common C. local D regional] language of a state, you would be able to find more fine-grained maps that map traditional minority languages into a particular territory. Language maps do not only inform us about global language distributions; they also fulfil a __12__ [A. interactive B. discursive C. intuitive D. reclusive] function, they establish a __13__ [A. plan B. wedge C. bridge D. link] between language and territory as a central and normal way to think about language use. The territorial __14__ [A. principle B. concept C. perception D. technique] is foundational to most thinking about linguistic justice because it __15__ [A. undergirds B. undermines C. underestimate D. underserved] linguistic legislation.

(Source: Ingrid Piler, 2016 p. 33, Linguistic Diversity and Social Justice. Oxford, Oxford University Press)
subject: English Language

section:
The passage below has gaps. Following each gap, four options are provided. Choose the most appropriate option for each gap.

One way in which we come to see monolingual standard languages as the norm and __6__ [A. deviations B. aberrations C. criticisms D. deviants] from the imagined monolingual standard language as aberrant, and remember each individual __7__ [A. repertoire B. word C. idea D. statement] is a deviation but repertoires characterised by greater deviation as problematic is through the close association between language and place. You would have seen maps that map languages into territory. You probably can __8__ [A. write B. set C. conjure D. let] in your mind a map of the Americas where almost all of North America is __9__ [A. known B.  coded C. accepted D. conceded] for English except for a bit of French in East Canada, more than half of Central and South America would be coded for Spanish, the other big chunk (Brazil) for Portuguese, and three tiny __10__ [A. havens B. colonies C. isles D. pockets] of English, Dutch and French where the states of Guyana, Suriname and French Guiana are located in addition to maps based on the __11__ [A. national B. common C. local D regional] language of a state, you would be able to find more fine-grained maps that map traditional minority languages into a particular territory. Language maps do not only inform us about global language distributions; they also fulfil a __12__ [A. interactive B. discursive C. intuitive D. reclusive] function, they establish a __13__ [A. plan B. wedge C. bridge D. link] between language and territory as a central and normal way to think about language use. The territorial __14__ [A. principle B. concept C. perception D. technique] is foundational to most thinking about linguistic justice because it __15__ [A. undergirds B. undermines C. underestimate D. underserved] linguistic legislation.

(Source: Ingrid Piler, 2016 p. 33, Linguistic Diversity and Social Justice. Oxford, Oxford University Press)
subject: English Language

question: Select the option that best fills the gap labelled 11
solution: This refers to maps that reflect the dominant language(s) of a nation.

question: Select the option that best fills the gap labelled 12
solution: Language maps not only show distributions but also shape discussions and ideologies about language use.

question: Select the option that best fills the gap labelled 13
solution: The maps establish a connection between language and territory.

question: Select the option that best fills the gap labelled 14
solution: The "territorial principle" is a foundational concept in discussions about linguistic justice, as it establishes rules or standards about territory and language.

question: Select the option that best fills the gap labelled 15
solution: The territorial principle "supports" or "forms the basis of" linguistic legislation. "Undergirds" conveys this meaning.

question:
Choose the option that best explains the information conveyed in the sentence.

She is travelling to Abuja. She'll return next week.
solution:
The sentence "She is travelling to Abuja. She'll return next week." conveys two separate actions:

  1. She is currently travelling to Abuja.
  2. She plans to return next week.

Option D combines both ideas in a grammatically correct way using "and" to join the two clauses, accurately reflecting the meaning.

options.a: She is travelling to Abuja to return next week
options.b: She is travelling to Abuja but returning next week
options.c: She is travelling to Abuja to returning next week
options.d: She is travelling to Abuja and returning next week

subject: English Language

question:
Select the option that best explains the information conveyed in the sentence

Our school field is bigger than yours.
solution: The sentence "Our school field is better than yours" implies a comparison in quality, size or condition between the two fields, with the speaker's school field being superior. The phrase "not as big as ours" accurately captures the intended comparison.

options.a: Your school is bigger than ours
options.b: Your school is not bigger than ours
options.c: Our school field is as big as yours
options.d: Your school field is not as big as ours

subject: English Language

question:
Choose the option that best explains the information conveyed in the sentence

The manager told his workers to call it a day.
solution: The phrase "call it a day" is an idiom that means to stop working for the rest of the day or to end an activity. In this context, the manager instructed the workers to conclude their work for the day.

options.a: They were told to continue working
options.b: They were told to intensify their efforts
options.c: They were told to stop working
options.d: They were told to pause for a few minutes

subject: English Language

question:
Choose the option that explains the information conveyed in the sentence

The teacher believed her students were equal to the task.
solution: The phrase "equal to the task" means being capable, competent or well-prepared to handle a challenge. This implies that the teacher had confidence in the students' abilities.

options.a: The students were young and weak
options.b: The students were hardworking and dedicated
options.c: The students were not adequately prepared
options.d: The task was very difficult to carry out

subject: English Language

question:
Choose the option opposite in meaning to the word or phrase underlined. 

There is no likeness between him and his brother.
solution: The word "likeness" means similarity or resemblance. The opposite of this is dissimilarity
subject: English Language

question:
Choose the option opposite in meaning to the word or phrase underlined. 

Where is the wealth the rich man accumulated in his lifetime?
solution: The word "accumulated" means to gather or collect over time. The opposite is "squandered," which means to waste or spend recklessly, leading to a loss instead of accumulation.
subject: English Language

question:
Choose the option opposite in meaning to the word or phrase underlined.

The cleaner admires the girls for their neatness.
solution: The word "admires" means to respect or hold someone in high regard. The opposite is "scorns," which means to show contempt or disdain.
subject: English Language

question:
Choose the option that is opposite in meaning to the word or phrase underlined.

The commissioner thought that the proposal would fall through.
solution: The phrase "fall through" means to fail or not materialise. The opposite of "fail" is "succeed."
subject: English Language

question:
Choose the option nearest in meaning to the word or phrase underlined.

His persistently poor performance is already driving his parents round the bend.
solution: The phrase "driving someone round the bend" is an idiomatic expression that means to cause frustration or distress. While "mad" (option B) might seem like a close option, "causing his parents some problems" is the most accurate choice in this context, as it suggests that the poor performance is creating difficulty for the parents.

options.c: driving his parents into a corner

subject: English Language

question:
Choose the option nearest in meaning to the word or phrase underlined.

We had to prevail upon him to accept that offer.
solution: The phrase "prevail upon" means to persuade or convince someone to do something. Therefore, persuade is the nearest in meaning to "prevail upon."
subject: English Language

question:
Choose the option nearest in meaning to the word or phrase underlined. 

Mr Jebu displayed a high sense of conviviality during the Gala.
solution: The word "conviviality" refers to a cheerful, friendly and sociable atmosphere. Therefore, friendliness is the nearest in meaning to "conviviality."
subject: English Language

question:
Choose the option nearest in meaning to the word or phrase underlined. 

Despite the cancellation of the excursion, the students still waited at the school gate.
solution: The word "cancellation" in this context refers to the ending or termination of an event, and "abortion" can be used to mean the act of stopping something before it is completed.
subject: English Language

question:
Choose the option nearest in meaning to the word or phrase underlined. 

Accountants are discreet with their records.
solution: The word "discreet" means showing careful judgment, especially in maintaining privacy or confidentiality. Cautious is the nearest in meaning, as it implies being careful and prudent, similar to being discreet.
subject: English Language

question:
Choose the option that best completes the gap(s)

Fierce dogs should be kept in the _______ during the day.
solution: Dogs are usually kept in kennels.
subject: English Language

question:
Choose the option that best completes the gap(s)

We have known Rose for some years now, she is a friend of ____
solution: This is the correct form of the possessive pronoun and indicates something that belongs to "us." Possessive pronouns do not require an apostrophe.
subject: English Language

question:
Choose the option that best completes the gap(s)

The new national security committee ____ retired military personnel.
solution: The verb comprises means "includes" or "consists of" and does not require the preposition "of."
subject: English Language

question:
Choose the option that best completes the gap(s)

His car ____ of fuel so he arrived late for the meeting.
solution: The phrase ran out is correctly used to show that his car was depleted of fuel, which caused the delay. Using "ran out of" would lead to the repetition of the preposition "of".
subject: English Language

question:
Choose the option that best complete(s) the gap.

________ the word in the Thesaurus!
solution: The phrase look up is the appropriate phrasal verb used when searching for information in a reference source, such as a Thesaurus. The phrase "look for" is also appropriate as it means to search for something physically or mentally. However, the phrasal verb "look up" is more appropriate for this context.
subject: English Language

question:
Choose the option that best completes the gap(s)

Ade was told to finish the assignment ____ time.
solution: The phrase on time means completing something at or before the expected or scheduled time.
subject: English Language

question:
Choose the option that best completes the gap(s)

_________ is expanding rapidly.
solution: "The city of Abuja" is the correct phrase because it uses the definite article "The," which is necessary when referring to a specific, known city.
subject: English Language

question:
Choose the option that has the same vowel sound as the one represented by the letter(s) underlined.

buoy
solution:
buoy - /bɔɪ/

boy - /bɔɪ/

trough - /trɒf/

route -  /ruːt/

good - /ɡʊd/
subject: English Language

question:
Choose the option that has the same vowel sound as the one represented by the letter(s) underlined. 

money
solution:
money - /ˈmʌn.i/

Monday -  /ˈmʌn.deɪ/

go -  /ɡəʊ/

mouth -  /maʊθ/

rock - /rok/
subject: English Language

question:
Choose the option that has the same consonant sound as the one represented by the letter(s) underlined.

bet
solution:
bet - /bet/

pap - /pæp/

debt - /det/

tomb - /tuːm/

lab - /læb/
subject: English Language

question:
Choose the option that has the same consonant sound as the one represented by the letter(s) underlined.

river
solution:
river -  /ˈrɪv.ə/

fear -  /fɪə/

car - /kɑː/

risk - /rɪsk/

litre - /ˈliː.tə/
subject: English Language

question:
Choose the most appropriate stress pattern from the options. The stressed syllables are written in capital letters. 

Informative
solution: For polysyllabic words that end in -ive, the stress is usually placed on the antepenultimate syllable. Therefore, in the word "informative", the stress should be placed on "for".
subject: English Language

question:
The word in capital letters has the emphatic stress. Choose the option to which the given sentence relates.

The PRODUCT was rejected as fake.
solution: Emphatic stress is the placement of greater stress on a particular word or group of words in an utterance. This is usually done for emphasis or clarification. The question requires you to find the question that relates to the sentence given. The sentence is meant to clarify an error that was made in this question. The emphasised word, therefore, should be different in the question. This is why the answer is option D. "Was the produce rejected as fake?" No, "The PRODUCT was rejected as fake."

options.b: Was the product rejected though genuine?
options.d: Was the produce rejected as fake?

subject: English Language

question: What was the common ritual at the morning assembly at Stardom on Tuesdays and Thusdays?
solution: On Tuesdays and Thursdays, Stardom Schools recited the second stanza of the national anthem: "Oh God of creation, direct our noble cause..." This can be found on page 6 of the novel.

options.c: The second stanza of the national anthem.

subject: English Language

question: How much was the boarding house fee per session at Stardom before it was reduced?
solution: The boarding fee per session was N250,000 before it was reduced to N165,000. This information can be found on page 6 of the novel.

options.a: one hundred and sixty three thousand naira
options.b: two hundred and fifty thousand naira.
options.c: one hundred and sixty thousand naira.

subject: English Language

question: Who instructed the Chemistry teacher to conclude the assembly after Mr Bepo burst into tears?
solution: The VP, Mrs Apeh, instructed the Chemistry teacher, Mr Justus Anabel, to conclude the national assembly. This can be found on page 5 of the novel.
subject: English Language

question: Why did the Vice Principal contact the MD after Mr Bepo’s crying incident at the assembly?
solution: The VP's phone rang incessantly as she tried to console Mr Bepo. THe calls were from worried parents who had heard different rumours about the incident. Mrs Apeh, at that point, realised the best she could do was to call the MD. This can be found on page 6 of the novel.

options.b: Because parents were calling with concerns.

subject: English Language

question: The principal was nicknamed "The Lekki Headmaster" because...
solution: Mr Bepo was nicknamed The Lekki Headmaster because of the way he used to imitate characters in the old TV drama, Village Headmaster, when he was Headmaster at Stardom Kiddies. This can be found on page 8 of the novel.

options.a: He lived in Lekki during his time as a headmaster.
options.b: He humorously imitated characters from the Village Headmaster.
options.c: He introduced a new curriculum in the school.

subject: English Language

question: Which teachers were reprimanded because two of their candidates had Ds in their subjects?
solution: The only teachers reprimanded because two candidates had Ds in their subjects were Mr. Obong Ukaku ad Miss Taye Kareem. These teachers taught Chemistry and Geography respectively. This can be found on page 8 of the novel.

options.a: Mr. Ope Wande and Mrs. Grace Apeh.
options.b: Mr. Audu and Mr. Justus Anabel.
options.c: Mr. Obong Ukake and Miss Taye Kareem.
options.d: Mr. Bepo and Mr. Ope Wande.

subject: English Language

question: Who among the staff at Stardom School is also a pastor?
solution: Mr Ope Wande, the Physics teacher, is also a pastor. This can be found on page 9 of the novel.
subject: English Language

question: What was given as a reward to teachers whose students scored distinctions in their subjects?
solution: The teachers who handled subjects where candidates scored distinctions took home 30,000 each. This can be found on page 8 of the novel.
subject: English Language

question: Why did the MD decide to send the principal home after the crying incident?
solution: The MD instructed Mr Bepo to be taken home because she felt pressed to move the drama away from the school as soon as possible. She was worried about what the parents would think if they learned that the principal was weeping like a child. This can be found on page 9 of the novel.

options.b: To avoid disturbing the students further.
options.c: To allow the principal to rest.

subject: English Language

question: Who accompanied the principal to his home on the day of the crying incident?
solution: The guidance counsellor accompanied Mr Bepo home on the day of the crying incident, as instructed by the MD. This can be found on page 9 of the novel.
subject: English Language

question: What was the student Ikenna Egbu’s speech about?
solution: Ikenna's speech is about his recent excursion to Jos. He described Jos as a brilliantly beautiful city. This can be found on page 7 of the novel.

options.a: The “Japa” trend in our modern society.
options.b: The principal’s contributions to the school.

subject: English Language

question: Why is Mr. Bepo reluctant to relocate to the United Kingdom?
solution: Mr Bepo is reluctant to relocate to the United Kingdom because he very much loved Stardom Schools, especially is students. He cared about them and wanted to continually be part of their growth. This ca be found on page 10 of the novel.

options.a: He does not want to leave his students.
options.b: He is afraid of adapting to a new environment.
options.c: He believes life in Nigeria is better.
options.d: He does not want to leave his wife and children behind.

subject: English Language

question: What trait earned Mr. Bepo the nickname “The Lekki Headmaster”?
solution: Mr Audu joked about Bepo's disposition saying: "The way the headmaster masterly resolves these fights reminds me of the way King Oloja, in Village Headmaster, resolved all the roforofos brought by his chiefs and villages. So, as they had their own Village Headmaster, we also have our own Lekki Headmaster." This can be found on page 10 of the novel.

options.c: His ability to resolve conflicts amicably

subject: English Language

question: Who coined the nickname “The Lekki Headmaster”?
solution: Mr Audu coined the name by comparing him to King Oloja from Village Headmaster. This can be found on page 10 of the novel.
subject: English Language

question: How much does Seri earn monthly as a nurse in the United Kingdom?
solution: His wife, Seri, was a nurse who, according to rumours, was earning up to 10,000 pounds per month. This can be found on page 11 of the novel.
subject: English Language

question: What was Mr. Bepo’s monthly salary at Stardom Schools?
solution: As a principal, Mr Bepo's salary was about N400,000. This can be found on page 11 of the novel.
subject: English Language

question: Why do Mr. Bepo’s colleagues find his reluctance to relocate amusing?
solution: The staff at Stardom Schools find his reluctance to relocate amusing because they believe he should jump at the opportunity to earn abroad. They did the comparison of the amount earned both in Nigeria and abroad and resolved that it would be more profitable to relocate abroad. This can be found on page 11 of the novel.

options.a: They think he is afraid of flying.
options.b: They assume he wants to stay in Nigeria to fulfil his entrepreneurial dreams.
options.c: They think he dislikes the United Kingdom.
options.d: They believe he should jump at the opportunity to earn more abroad.

subject: English Language

question: Why did the Fruitful Future school fail?
solution: Fruitful Future school failed because the roads around the school began to depreciate due to government negligence, as a result, most of the parents started to relocate. The parents that were left could not afford the school fees This can be found on page 39 of the novel.

options.b: Bepo was not willing to be involved in the ritual practices in the area

subject: English Language

question: Which factor makes Mr. Bepo cautious about entering the commercial transportation business?
solution: Mr Bepo was wary about entering the commercial transportation business venture because the drivers were untrustworthy. They either frustrated the idea, defaulted on payment, or colluded with mechanics to defraud the vehicle owners. This can be found on page 12 of the novel.

options.d: Lack of interest in the sector

subject: English Language

question: What does the term roforofos that Mr Audu used when talking about Mr Bepo most likely refer to?
solution: The term roforofos as used by Mr Audu on page 10 of the novel refers to "petty disagreements and conflicts". These were the conflicts that King Oloja, in Village Headmaster, tried to resolve.
subject: English Language

question: What does Mr. Bepo’s reluctance to relocate reveal about his values?
solution: The fact that Mr Bepo was reluctant to relocate abroad despite the financial, familial and professional benefits that he would obtain if he did so shows that he valued his students and their growth over all else.

options.a: He values financial security over personal fulfilment.
options.b: He prioritises his students and professional relationships.
options.d: He feels unprepared for a new environment.

subject: English Language

question: According to Bepo, why is the hourly payment system beneficial?
solution: Bepo believes that the hourly system of payment is much more beneficial to the employees than the monthly system.  This is because it would make hob schedules flexible and offer the employee an opportunity to change jobs as often as he could. This can be found on page14 of the novel.

options.a: It allows employees to earn more.
options.b: It enables employees to change jobs easily
options.c: It reduces the workload for employees.
options.d: It allows employees to take more holidays.

subject: English Language

question: According to the novel, what is the approximate annual migration rate of Nigerian doctors?
solution: According to the novel, up to 1000 out of the country's 3000 doctors were migrating annually. This can be found on page 15 of the novel.
subject: English Language

question: What unethical act did Mr. Nku engage in before relocating abroad?
solution: Mr Nku, one of the staff at Stardom Schools, took a N2 million loan from the school's cooperative and relocated abroad two days later. This can be found on page 15 of the novel.

options.b: He borrowed N2 million from the school’s cooperative
options.c: He falsified documents to gain a visa.
options.d: He stole money from the school’s treasury.

subject: English Language

question: Why did one of the school drivers attempt to sell the school bus?
solution: One of the school's drivers attempted to steal the bus in his custody because he wanted to sell it and sue the proceeds to send his son to college abroad. This can be found on page 15 of the novel.

options.a: To pay his son’s tuition fees abroad
options.d: To finance a trip to the UK

subject: English Language

question: According to the novel, why do most Nigerians prefer relocating to the UK?
solution: Accordig to the novel, People in the UK understand that there is an opportunity for their children to enjoy quality education and the healthcare system is much better than in Nigeria. This can be found on page 16 and 17 of the novel.

options.c: Free education and healthcare for their children
options.d: Higher job opportunities compared to other countries

subject: English Language

question: What proverb does Bepo recall from his Idoma co-tenant?
solution: Bepo remembers this proverb when he realises that people always have different results when they relocate abroad. This proverb can be found on page 17 of the novel.

options.a: “Whether the employer records gains or not, the employee will yet take home his full pay.”
options.c: “Life’s intriguing tests yield the same results for everyone.”
options.d: “The sugarcane and the bitter leaf get different tastes from the same rain.”

subject: English Language

question: What lesson does Hope’s story about relocating to the UK teach?
solution: Hope was an accountant who applied for and secured admission to a Master's in the UK. His wife joined him to work and earn the money that would see him through. But barely four months later, his wife stopped providing for him. She explained that the demands of her job made it impracticable to keep sponsoring him. This teaches the importance of financial independence when relocating abroad This can be found on page 17 of the novel.

options.a: Financial independence is critical when relocating.
options.c: Couples always succeed when they share responsibilities.
options.d: Relocation is a foolproof plan for success.

subject: English Language

question: How much did Sola and her husband borrow to fund their relocation?
solution: Sola and her husband had to borrow about N4 million to fund their relocation. This can be found on page 16 of the novel.
subject: English Language

question: Where does Bepo live?
solution: Bepo lives at Adeniyi Jones, Ikeja, Lagos. This can be found on page 19 of the novel.
subject: English Language

question: Why does Mr Ignatius want to relocate with his family?
solution: Mr Ignatius does not want his children to grow up in Nigeria. He said: "I want them to grow up in a sane environment. I don't want other children that are being trained outside the country now to return and lord things over my own children." This can be found on page 20 of the novel.

options.b: To secure a better future for his children.
options.c: To reunite with his extended family abroad.
options.d: To enrol his children in Stardom schools abroad.

subject: English Language

question: What skill did Mrs Ignatius begin learning to support her family abroad?
solution: Mrs Ignatius, in preparation to relocate abroad learnt tailoring and hairdressing. This can be found on page 20 of the novel.
subject: English Language

question: What disrupted the Ignatius family's relocation plans?
solution: During the visa processing, a DNA test reveals that Mr Ignatius is not the father of Favour, one of the children. As a result, the family never remained the same again. This can be found on page 20 of the novel.

options.b: Mrs Ignatius changed her mind about relocating.
options.c: A DNA test revealed Mr Ignatius was not Favour's biological father.

subject: English Language

question: Why was Mr Ayesoro transferred from his teaching role?
solution: One of Mr Ayesoro's students, Bibi had recurring nightmares about him because of his scary tribal marks. When Bibi;s mother laid a complaint at the school, the management transferred Mr Ayesoro to Stardom Hub, the property wing of Stardom Group of Companies. This can be found on page 22 and 23 of the novel.

options.b: A student had recurring nightmares about him.
options.c: He requested a transfer for personal reasons.
options.d: The school wanted him to work in their property division.

subject: English Language

question: What nickname did students give to Mr Ayesoro because of his tribal marks?
solution: Mr Ayesore was nicknamed Mr Owala because of his scary tribal mark. This can be found on page 22 of the novel.
subject: English Language

question: Why did the school transfer Mr Ayesoro to Stardom Hub?
solution: In The Lekki Headmaster, Mr. Ayesoro was transferred to Stardom Hub as part of the school's effort to keep Mrs. Ladele’s children enrolled. The school administration made this decision because Mrs. Ladele, a wealthy and influential parent, had made it clear that she would withdraw her children if Mr. Ayesoro was not transferred to the new branch. This can be found on page 23 of the novel.

options.b: To provide him with a better job opportunity.
options.c: To reward him for his dedication.
options.d: To retain Mrs Ladele’s children in the school.

subject: English Language

question: What does the MD discover about the land Stardom acquired two years ago?
solution: While Mrs Ibbidunn Gloss was taking a walk on the school property, she chose to check a piece of land that was acquired by the school two years ago. This is when she discovered that the staff at Stardom were using that piece of land as a car park even after the staff had been assigned parks within the premises. This can be found on page 24 and 25 of the novel.

options.a: It is being used for illegal activities.
options.b: It has been sold without her knowledge.
options.c: Teachers and staff are using it as a car park.
options.d: It is being developed into a new school building.

subject: English Language

question: How are the teachers and staff at Stardom able to afford cars?
solution: The staff at Stardom Schools are able to afford cars because they take loans from the school's cooperative society. This can be found on page 25 and 26 of the novel where the principal reveals this to the MD after she found the cars parked in one of the school's properties that they have turned into a car park.

options.b: They have taken loans from the school’s cooperative society.

subject: English Language

question: How much money is in the account of the Stardom Cooperative Society, as discovered by the board?
solution: The amount of money in the cooperative's purse is N95 million, while over N50 million had been loaned out. This can be found on page 26 of the novel.
subject: English Language

question: What simile does the Chairman use to describe the potential risk of the cooperative funds?
solution: This can be found on page 26 of the novel.

options.a: It’s like building castles in the air.
options.b: It’s like hanging a snake in the roof and going to bed.
options.c: It’s like lighting a fire in the middle of a haystack.
options.d: It’s like giving a sword to your enemy.

subject: English Language

question: What decision does the board make about loans from the cooperative?
solution: The board resolved that no staff member could borrow more than 250,000. This can be found on page 26 of the novel.

options.a: No loans can be given to staff under any circumstances.
options.b: All loan requests must be approved by the staff union.
options.d: The cooperative must stop lending money to staff immediately.

subject: English Language

question: Why do some teachers at Stardom Schools dread Open Day?
solution: This can be found on page 27 of the novel where Openn Day is said to be dreaded by teachers because parents often came with "trailer-loads of complaints."

options.a: They are afraid of being sacked
options.d: The day involves long hours of work

subject: English Language

question: What did Mr Guta demand in the MD’s office regarding Mr Fafore?
solution: Mr Guta asked the MD to immediately sack Mr Fafore for the supposed "grammatical error" that Mr Fafore had taught his child. This can be found on page 27 of the novel.

options.a: A salary increase for Mr Fafore
options.b: The immediate dismissal of Mr Fafore
options.c: A change in his son’s class teacher.
options.d: A public apology from Mr Fafore.

subject: English Language

question: Why does the school no longer allow students to copy notes from the Class Prefect?
solution: The school no longer permitted students to copy notes from the class prefect because a parent once sued the school for this practice. The parent, an accountant with an oil company did not like how his daughter was exploited by the teachers as she was told to copy the notes on the board because she had good handwriting. This can be found on page 29 of the novel.

options.b: A parent sued the school for this practice
options.c: The students often make errors when copying notes
options.d: The teachers believe it wastes time

subject: English Language

question: What reason did Mr Guta give for being angry with Mr Fafore?
solution: Mr Guta was angry with Mr Fafore and wanted to get him fired simply because he spotted a supposed "grammatical error" in his son's note. It is eventually discovered that what he found was not a grammatical error but a properly constructed expression. This entire incident can be found in chapter six of the novel.

options.a: He found a grammatical error in his son's note
options.b: He felt Mr Fafore was not teaching effectively
options.c: He was unhappy with Mr Fafore's attitude
options.d: His son complained about being treated unfairly

subject: English Language

question: What grammatical rule was the source of disagreement between the MD and the principal?
solution: The grammatical rule that was the source of disagreement is the "subject-verb agreement rule" with the expression "Ade as well as Jide comes vs. come." Mr Guta and the MD believed that Mr Fafore had taught the children the wrong thing when he instructed his students to write "Ade as well as Jide comes" in their notes. This was the reason Mr Fafore was sacked. However, the principal reveals to the MD later on that this expression is indeed accurate because when the construction "as well as" is used to join two noun subjects together, the singular verb (comes) is used. This can be found in chapter six of the novel.

options.a: The use of the subjunctive mood
options.b: The use of conjunctions such as (and)
options.c: The subject-verb agreement with phrases like (as well as)

subject: English Language

question: What did Mr Audu do after the MD felt deflated that she had wrongly sacked Mr Fafore?
solution: Mr Audu, the school's fine art teacher, and "clown" of the novel, in is usual fashion, cracked a joke to lighten the tense atmosphere brought on by the sad realisation that Mr Fafore had been wrongly sacked. He says "I just want to say that Mr Fafore,... as well as the principal ... is correct. And the MD, Mrs Ibidun Gloss, is hereby pardoned, discharged and acquitted." (The Lekki Headmaster: pg. 33).

options.a: He defended Mr Fafore's teaching methods
options.b: He proposed new rules for grammar checks
options.c: He suggested a training session for the staff
options.d: He cracked a joke to lighten the mood

subject: English Language

question: What conclusion did the teachers draw from Mr Fafore's sack incident?
solution: Mr Fafore's sack incident reminded the teachers at Stardom School of the essential fact that there is no job security in the establishment. This was after Mr Fafore had come into the staff room to pack up his personal items. This can be found on page 31 of the novel.

options.a: Open Day is a stressful event
options.b: There is no job security in the establishment
options.c: Parents should not interfere with teaching methods
options.d: The MD is always fair in her decisions

subject: English Language

question: Why did Bepo leave Beesway Group of School?
solution: Bepo left Beesway Group of School after witnessing the ritual that occurred in the school's compound. He considered this practice ungodly and chose to leave the school. This can be found in chapter 7 of the novel.

options.a: He was accused of sharing confidential information with the parents
options.b: He was dissatisfied with the grammatical error in the the school's name
options.c: He witnessed an event that he considered ungodly
options.d: He disagreed with the director about his salary

subject: English Language

question: What grammatical issue did Bepo raise about Beesway Group of School's name?
solution: The grammatical issue that Bepo had a problem with in the school's name was the use of singular in "Group of School". He tries to get the director to understand that when a collective noun like "group" is used, it should be followed by a plural noun as in "Group of Schools". This can be found on page 34 of the novel.

options.b: The use of singular instead of plural in (Group of School)
options.c: The absence of a hyphen in the name
options.d: The misuse of capital letters in the name

subject: English Language

question: Why did the director of "Beesway Group of School" claim the ritual involving the cow was necessary?
solution: The director claimed the ritual was necessary because it was actually part of a special prayer for his late father, who had given him the land on which the school was built. This can be found on page 41 of the novel.

options.a: To pray for his late father who gave him the land
options.b: To bring in more pupils to the school
options.c: To appease the parents who had raised complaints
options.d: To conduct a purification ceremony for the school

subject: English Language

question: What led to the closure of the school Bepo started?
solution: Bepo's own school, "Fruitful Future School" was shut down because a lot of the parents moved out of the neighbourhood because the condition of the roads was worsening and the government did nothing about it. The parents that were left in the neighbourhood could not afford the fees and so Bepo had to shut the school down. This can be found on page 39 of the novel.

options.a: Poor road conditions in the neighbourhood
options.c: A failed spiritual ritual to attract more pupils

subject: English Language

question: What was the cost of the form for the position of Head Boy or Head Girl in the school election at Stardom?
solution: The cost of the form for the position of Head Boy or Head Girl is N50,000. This can be found on page 41 of the novel.
subject: English Language

question: What inappropriate remark did Banky make during Speech Day?
solution: During Speech Day, Banky said: "Instead of voting for the son of an ex-convict, cast your vote for me...". This can be found on page 42 of the novel.

options.a: Tosh was unqualified for the position
options.b: Tosh was the son of an ex-convict
options.c: Tosh bribed his way into the election
options.d: Tosh lacked the required skill for the role

subject: English Language

question: Why was Tosh's father, Chief Ogba, detained for 36 months?
solution: Tosh's father, Chief Didi Ogba spent 36 months in detention because he was facing trial for alleged misappropriation of a 2.5 billion government contract sum. This can be found on page 42 of the novel.

options.b: He failed to pay a school debt
options.c: He was accused of fraud but acquitted later
options.d: He faced trial for alleged misappropriation

subject: English Language

question: Which of the following waterfalls is the highest in West Africa, according to the novel?
solution: According to the novel, Owu waterfalls in Kwara is the highest in West Africa, measuring 120 meters above water level and cascading 330 feet down an escarpment. This can be found on page 44 of the novel.
subject: English Language

question: What is the primary reason Mr Bepo organises excursions for the students at Stardom?
solution: Bepo organised a lot of excursions for the students because he wanted them to have a "thorough grasp of their country." He knew that most of the students came from elite families that would eventually send them abroad after secondary education. This can be found on page 44 of the novel.

options.a: To give them a good grasp of their country before they all go abroad.
options.b: To promote Stardom as a school that values excursions
options.c: To prepare students for geography exams.

subject: English Language

question:
Which of the following festivals have the students attended? 

i. Calabar Carnival

ii. Osun Oshogbo Festival

iii. Argungu Festival

iv. Abuja Carnival
solution: The students have attended the Calabar, Osun Osogbo and Argungu festivals. The Abuja festival was not included in the novel. This can be found on page 45 of the novel.
subject: English Language

question: How did Mr Bepo inspire students after they visited areas like Mushin and Ajegunle during their excursions?
solution: The students, during their excursion through places like Mushin, Ajegunle and Ijora, derided the area but Mr Bepo cautioned against that saying that being born in such areas does not determine one's success in life. He mentions successful people who once lived in slums but are now successful and live in places like Banana Island because they made use of their talents, money and opportunities. He mentioned people like Odion Ighalo and Victor Osimhen who grew up "in the low ends of Lagos." This can be found on page 46 of the novel.

options.a: By promising to relocate them to better areas
options.b: By sharing stories of successful individuals who rose from slums
options.c: By discouraging them from visiting such areas again
options.d: By giving them motivational books about success

subject: English Language

question: What was the source of the name "Badagry", according to the novel?
solution: The name Badagry originated from "Agbadarigi". This can be found on page 47 of the novel.

options.a: It was named after the Badagry River
options.c: It originated from a European explorer's name
options.d: It was named after a nearby slave market

subject: English Language

question: What does Mr Bepo mean by the term "new slavery"?
solution: The "new slavery" that Bepo refers to is "Africans themselves..., in Japa fit, voluntarily and desperately walking into the workforce of who seemed to be the yester masters." (The Lekki Headmaster: pg. 47)

options.a: Africans willingly move abroad for better opportunities
options.b: The continuation of slave trade in some regions
options.c: The exploitation of Africans by modern companies
options.d: Africans selling themselves into physical labour

subject: English Language

question: Why was Mr Bepo moved during his visit to the Black Heritage Museum?
solution: At the Black Heritage Museum, Bepo was emotionally moved by the memory of the ugly experiences the enslaved went through. This can be found on page 47 of the novel.

options.a: He felt nostalgic about Nigeria's history
options.b: He was impressed by the museum's architecture
options.c: He empathised with the suffering of slaves in the past
options.d: He remembered his ancestors were involved in slavery

subject: English Language

question: What is the meaning of the term "japa" as used in the novel?
solution: According to the novel, "to japa" means to relocate abroad in search of better opportunities. This idea is the central subject matter in the novel.

options.b: To travel abroad in search of better opportunities
options.c: To resist colonial influences inn modern times
options.d: To work as a slave in a foreign country

subject: English Language

question: Why was Mr Bepo initially reluctant to renew his passport?
solution: The passage states that Bepo had not been keen on relocating abroad, which is the main reason for his initial reluctance to renew his passport. Since he wasn't planning on traveling abroad, he didn't prioritize renewing his passport.

options.b: He was too busy with his teaching duties
options.c: He had heard about the hassles applicants were facing
options.d: He did not plan to travel abroad anytime soon

subject: English Language

question: How much was the official fee for a 10-year passport renewal (64 pages)?
solution: This can be found on page 49 of the novel.
subject: English Language

question: Why did Bepo choose Ibadan for his passport renewal?
solution: Bepo knew that travelling to neighbouring states like Ogun, Oyo, Osun and Kwara would make the passport renewal easier and faster as there would be fewer crowds than in Lagos so he settled to renew his passport in Ibadan, Oyo State. This can be found on page 48 of the novel.

options.a: It was closer to his location
options.b: He was advised that the process would be easier there
options.c: He had a friend who worked in immigration there
options.d: The fees were lower in Ibadan

subject: English Language

question: What can be inferred about Tai's role in the passport renewal process?
solution: According to the novel, Tai was no immigrant personnel. He was just an ordinary business centre operator working in cahoots with some immigration staff. While some would call the guy a consultant, others might brand him a recketeer. This can be found on page 52 of the novel.

options.a: He is a middleman exploiting applicants
options.b: He was a legitimate immigration officer
options.c: He was an honest businessman offering his services
options.d: He was unaware of the actual renewal process

subject: English Language

question: What does the phrase "in cahoots" most likely mean in the context of Tai and the immigration staff?
solution: To be "in cahoots" with someone means to "secretly collaborate" with them, especially for the purpose of deceiving and exploiting others.
subject: English Language

question: Why does Mr Bepo delay his flight to the UK?
solution: Mr Bepo had to alter his departure date so that he could attend the farewell celebration planned for him by the school. The school agreed to pay the $100 fine that came with the alteration. This can e found on page 54 of the novel.

options.b: He is required to attend a farewell celebration
options.c: He needs to finalise his resignation paperwork
options.d: He has a last-minute meeting with the MD

subject: English Language

question: What Yoruba adage does Mr Bepo recall after the debate at the farewell celebration?
solution: This can be found on page 55 of the novel.

options.a: A wise man never leaves his work unfinished
options.b: The work of a teacher outlives their time
options.c: Success is not final; failure is not fatal
options.d: Even if the master carver retires, his carvings remain

subject: English Language

question: Why does Mr Bepo yell "Noooo!" during the drama club performance at the farewell celebration?
solution: While the drama class presented in front of Mr Bepo, they presented a Canoe dance of the Badagry people. This dance transported him back to Badagry where he had pictured the slaves and their agonies. This can be found on page 55 and 56 of the novel.

options.a: He was disappointed with the dance
options.b: He is overwhelmed by memories of the Heritage Slave Museum
options.c: He feels the performance is disrespectful
options.d: He disagrees with the students' portrayal of history

subject: English Language

question: What was the farewell gift presented to Mr Bepo?
solution: This can be found on page 58 of the novel.

options.a: A framed picture of the staff and students
options.b: A plaque commemorating his years of service
options.d: A plane ticket to his new destination

subject: English Language

question: At what time is Mr Bepo's flight to the UK scheduled to depart?
solution: Bepo was billed to jet out with Emirates Airlines at 10:00pm on Saturday. This can be found on page 59 of the novel.
subject: English Language

question: Who offers to drive Mr Bepo to the airport?
solution: Mr Ogunwale, Mr Bepo's landlord offered to drive him to the airport. This can be found on page 60 of the novel.
subject: English Language

question: What did Mr Bepo's wife insist he should pack for his trip to the UK?
solution: His wife had advised Mr Bepo to pack iru (locust beans) and egusi (melon seeds) ground with crayfish and she also requested that he buy dry snails. This can be found on page 60 of the novel.

options.a: Locust beans, egusi, ground crayfish and dry snail

subject: English Language

question: What is the primary setting of The Lekki Headmaster​​​​​​?
solution: The primary setting of the novel is Stardom Schools. This is where most of the events take place and it is also the source of Mr Bepo's conflict i.e. whether to keep working there as he has for more than two decades or to relocate abroad to join his wife and children.
subject: English Language

question: Who is the protagonist of the novel?
solution: Mr Adewale Adebbepo aka Bepo aka The Lekki Headmaster is the protagonist of the novel. It is around him that the events in the novel are centred.
subject: English Language

question: What is Mr Bepo's profession?
solution: Mr Bepo is the principal at Stardom Schools, Lekki.
subject: English Language

question: What is the main challenge that Mr Bepo faces in the novel?
solution: The central challenge that Mr Bepo faces is the intense pressure to migrate to the UK even when he wants to stay at Stardom School to finish the good work he started. The novel begins with the dramatic incident at the morning assembly where he bursts into tears when he is reminded of the fact that he will be leaving for the UK soon. The story ends with his return to Stardom Schools after he made the decision not to go to the UK.

options.a: Convincing parents to enrol their children
options.c: Balancing his career with pressure to migrate

subject: English Language

question: What theme is predominantly explored in the novel?
solution: The predominant theme in the novel is migration and identity. The author uses Mr Bepo's experiences as well as stories of migrants to other countries including the UK, Canada and USA to explore how the good and bad sides of relocating abroad and the resulting question of identity.
subject: English Language

question: Which teacher is known for the use of witty remarks in the novel?
solution: Mr Audu, the Fina Arts teacher, is known for his well-timed witty remark. In chapter 1 of the novel, he refers to the MD as "a witch and a wizard rolled into one". He is also the one who coined the name "The Lekki Headmaster" for Mr Bepo.
subject: English Language

question: Which two students have a rivalry that dates back to JSS 3?
solution: Banky and Tosh are SSS2 students who have a rivalry that dates back to JSS 3. Both students clashed in the Best Dancer category at the end-of-year party but Banky was crowned winner. This led Tosh's to threaten a lawsuit because they felt slighted. This can be found on page 42 of the novel.
subject: English Language

question: The novel explores the effects of migration under which term?
solution: The central theme in the novel is one of migration. The novel explores how people "japa" to other more developed countries in search of greener pastures.
subject: English Language

question: What led to the conflict during the prefect election speech?
solution: The conflict arose during Speech Day when Banky said: "Instead of voting for the son of an ex-convict, cast your vote for me..." This can be found on page 42 of the novel.

options.b: Banky attempted to bribe the judges
options.c: Tosh's mom accused the school of favouritism
options.d: Bepo intervened in the voting process

subject: English Language

question: What project was the school's Invention Club working on?
solution: The school's Invention Club was working on a phone-making initiative. This can be found on page 43 of the novel.
subject: English Language

question: What significant historical location did the students visit in Badagry?
solution: The students at stardom school visited the First Storey Building in Nigeria, the Slave Market and the Point of No Return, the Seriki Abass Slave Museum as well as other historical sights in Badagry. This can be found on page 46 of the novel.
subject: English Language

question: What tourist attractions in Bauchi did the students explore?
solution: The Yakari Games Reserve is located in Bauchi. It is one of the locations that the Stardom students have been to. This can be found on page 45 of the novel.
subject: English Language

question: What was the major challenge that Bepo faced during his NIN validation?
solution: Bepo had a nasty experience at the NIN office because the network glitches were terrible. This can be found on page 53 of the novel.
subject: English Language

question: What performances during the send-off caused Bepo to become deeply emotional?
solution: The Canoe dance is a dance of the Badagry people. The performance reminds him of his time at the Heritage Museum in Badagry and he his reminded of the brutal treatment of slaves by the white men. This makes him emotional. This can be found on page 55-56 of the novel.
subject: English Language

question: What year was the Ikogosi Warm Sprigs discovered?
solution: This can be found on page 44 of the novel.
subject: English Language

question: What lesson does the novel teach about migration?
solution: The "migration tales" in the novel show that migration is a mixed bag of blessings and challenges. While some enjoy the benefits of the countries they relocated to, others like Hope face the bad side of migration.

options.a: Migration should always be the last option
options.b: Migration brings mixed blessings and challenges
options.d: Migration is a betrayal of one's roots

subject: English Language

question: What was Bepo's relationship with Mrs Ibidun Gloss?
solution:
Mrs. Ibidun Gloss was the Managing Director (MD) of Stardom Schools, which means she was Mr. Bepo’s direct supervisor. As the head of the school’s administration, she had the authority to oversee his work, make key decisions, and evaluate his performance.

While she respected and valued Mr. Bepo’s contributions, their relationship was first and foremost a professional one, where she had the final say on school matters. Therefore, the most accurate answer is that she was his supervisor rather than simply an admirer of his work.

options.c: She deeply respected and appreciated his contributions

subject: English Language

question: What caused Mr Bepo's emotional breakdown during the school assembly?
solution: Mr. Bepo’s breakdown was due to internal conflict and pressure to leave Stardom Schools for the UK. This can be found on page 10 of the novel where the reason for his breakdown is finally revealed.

options.c: His upcoming relocation to the UK

subject: English Language

question: What is the boarding fee per session at Stardom Schools?
solution: The boarding fee is N165,000 per session. This is a reduction from the original N250,000. This can be found on page 6 of the novel.
subject: English Language

question: Why was the boarding fee at Stardom Schools reduced?
solution: The boarding fee at Stardom school was reduced as a strategy to encourage punctuality among students. A lot of the students live very far away from school and so are not able to make it on time. The reduction of the boarding fees allowed a lot of parents to afford to enrol their students at the boarding houses. This can be found on page 6 of the novel.
subject: English Language

question: The theme central to the novel is
solution: The novel's central focus is the discussion of migration challenges and the crisis of identity. Bepo, as he prepares to relocate abroad, contemplates the different tales of those who migrated abroad. He also considered the impact this migration has on his identity. At the Heritage Museum in Badagry, he is reminded of the brutal history of slavery and feels that this voluntary migration to the lands of those who once colonised us is a form of "new slavery".

options.a: The corruption in the Nigerian education system
options.b: The struggles of students in private schools
options.c: The decline of traditional Nigerian values
options.d: The challenges of migration and identity

subject: English Language

question: How does The Lekki Headmaster portray the issue of "Japa"?
solution: The novel portrays the "Japa" issue as a personal and moral dilemma. The protagonist's journey throughout the novel is a personal journey. He contemplates every step he has made in both his personal and professional life to see how far he has come and to determine whether or not it would be a good idea to relocate abroad. Bepo understands that, on the one hand, travelling abroad has its benefits, such as increased pay, access to better healthcare and being closer to his family. But, he also understands that leaving Nigeria would mean abandoning his students whom he had nurtured. This is the personal and moral dilemma that most Nigerians face when they try to decide whether or not to relocate abroad.

options.a: As a necessary step for career growth
options.b: As a personal and moral dilemma
options.c: As a privilege for the wealthy
options.d: As an easy decision for most Nigerians

subject: English Language

question: What is the central physical setting of the novel?
solution: The central physical setting of the novel is Stardom Schools, Lagos. It is here that most of the events in the novel occur.
subject: English Language

question: Which setting in the novel serves as a point of psychological conflict for Bepo?
solution: The setting that serves as a source of psychological conflict for Bepo is the Black Heritage Museum in Badagry. Here, he contemplates the brutal history of black slaves and begins to draw a parallel between this history and the contemporary voluntary migration of Nigerians to countries of those who once colonised us. This becomes a source of psychological conflict for Bepo.
subject: English Language

question: What narrative technique is commonly used in The Lekki Headmaster?
solution: The most common narrative technique used in the novel is flashback. The writer uses this technique to relate events that happened in the past to the readers.
subject: English Language

question: What was Mr Bepo's long-term professional goal before considering migration?
solution:
This can be page 12 of the novel where it is revealed that Bepo, amongst other things, has thought of establishing his own school after having garnered a lot of experience in teaching and school management. His only fear was if he would have enough money to "float a standard school, given the taste he had already imbibed at Stardom." 

He had initially built a school - "Fruitful Future" but it had crumbled due to socio-economic challenges in the location.

options.a: Becoming the MD of Stardom Schools
options.c: Writing a book on educational reform
options.d: Retiring early and moving to his hometown

subject: English Language

question: What does Chief Ogba demand after his son, Tosh, is publicly insulted?
solution: Chief Ogba, Tosh's father, demanded that Banky and his parents write separate letters of apology to both him and his son. He also insisted that the letters be published in the school magazine. This was after Banky had called Tosh "the son of an ex-convict" during his speech for the position of Social prefect. This can be found on page 43 of the novel.

options.a: A formal apology and publication in the school magazine
options.b: The immediate suspension of the student who insulted his son
options.d: Immediate expulsion of the guilty student

subject: English Language

question: How does the novel use irony to critique migration?
solution: Irony is a powerful tool in the novel, used to critique societal norms and highlight the contradictions in modern Nigerian life. Bepo reflects on the irony of Africans willingly migrating to the countries of their former colonial masters, describing this as a “new slavery.”  The tales of Nigerians thriving or struggling abroad illustrate the irony of migration dreams. Stories like Jare’s regret over leaving a banking job to care for the elderly in London highlight the unpredictability of these pursuits.

options.a: It shows how people regret leaving Nigeria.
options.b: It portrays Nigerian schools as superior to foreign institutions.
options.c: It contrasts the excitement of moving abroad with the struggles people face when they get there.
options.d: It portrays that migration is always a failure.

subject: English Language

question: Why does the MD call an emergency meeting after discovering the cars at the school's land?
solution: The emergency meeting of the board of Directors was called because the MD was concerned that the Cooperative allowed the teachers to be financially independent. She believes that the staff could rebel and jointly establish their own school with the money and steal the school's band ideal. This can be found on page 26 of the novel.

options.a: She believes they are using stolen school funds.
options.b: She want to commend them for financial growth.
options.c: She plans to reduce the teachers' salaries.
options.d: She is concerned about the teachers becoming financially independent

subject: English Language

question: Who makes up the board of Stardom Schools?
solution: The Board of Directors at Stardom Schools is made up of the MD's family members: the chairman, Chief Mrs Solape Bayo is the MD's mother, Martins Bayo is a non-executive director; and Oya Bayo, the last born, was the board's secretary. This can be found on page 26 of the novel.

options.b: A mix of educators and business investors

subject: English Language

question: What did Bepo do before meeting with the director of Beesway the morning after he encountered the ritual practice?
solution: Bepo moved his belongings out of his room and the staff room the next morning before he went to see the director. This can be found on page 40 of the novel.

options.a: He informed the police about the ritual activities.
options.c: He moved his belongings out of the school.

subject: English Language

question: Which poem came to Bepo's mind on his way to renew his passport?
solution:
Bepo recalls the poem titled "Ibadan" by J.P. Clark as he journeyed through the capital city on his way to renew his passport. The poem gives an apt description of the Ibadan landscape that he sees as the Sienna he was in reached the top of the hilly bridge after Bere. 

Ibadan, grinning splash of rust

and gold-flung and scattered among 

seven hills like broken china in the sun. 

This can be found on page 51 of the novel.
subject: English Language

question: What was Mr. Fafore’s strategy to ensure that he came early to school even though he lives far away?
solution: Although Mr Fafore lives quite far away, he always prepares his day so that he arrives very early. He wakes up as early as 4:00 am and sets out for school at 4:30 am. He would get to the school premises around 6:00 am, rest his head on his desk and snooze for about an hour before his colleagues arrived. This can be found on page 30 of the novel.

options.a: He slept in his car overnight
options.c: He rented an apartment near the school
options.d: He set multiple alarms on his phone

subject: English Language

question: Which of the following is NOT one of Bepo's ideas about teaching?
solution: Bepo believes that learning should focus on the growth and development of the students. He focuses on imparting the knowledge that the students need to be able to thrive when they get out into the world. He also believes that learning should be hands-on and engaging as he constantly encourages doing extra-curriculars that help students apply the knowledge they learnt in practical situations. This can be seen in the phone-making initiative that he had hoped to see to the end before he relocated to the UK. Finally, he believes that excursions can enhance the learning experience. He took the students out on excursions so that they can have a solid knowledge of the Nigerian environment before they travelled abroad to further their education. However, he does not believe in maintaining a strict authoritarian approach. He rather believes in resolving conflicts amicably.

options.c: Teachers should maintain a strict authoritarian approach
options.d: Learning should be hands-on and engaging

subject: English Language

question: Why did Bepo choose to renew his passport in Ibadan?
solution: Bepo chose to renew his passport in Ibadan because there were longer queues in Lagos and paying to expedite the process could cost up to N200,000 to N250,000. He heard that he could travel to neighbouring states like Ogun, Oyo, Osun and Kwara where there would be less crowd; and so he settled for Ibadan. This can be found on page 48 of the novel.

options.a: It was more expensive to renew it in Lagos
options.b: He wanted to avoid the long queues in Lagos
options.d: He was transferred to the office in Ibadan

subject: English Language

question: Why did Stardom School stop the usual democratic election process for prefects?
solution: The usual democratic process of election of prefects was suspended at Stardom Schools because of the incident between Banky and Tosh. During Speech Day, Banky had said: "Instead of voting for the son of an ex-convict, cast your vote for me. You will never regret." He said this as part of his campaign and was directed at Tosh. This negative campaigning led to a chain of events including the suspension of this process of democratic election. This can be found on page 42 of the novel.

options.a: Some students were engaging in negative campaigning
options.b: The school wanted teachers to appoint the prefects instead.
options.c: The Ministry of Education mandated a new selection process
options.d: Parents demanded to be involved in selecting the prefects

subject: English Language

question: Why did Bepo spend the electricity tariff money he collected from his tenants?
solution: This is one of the most embarrassing actions he has undertaken, according to the novel. When Bepo was out of a job and had no savings or no new employment in sight, he spent the N2,500 electricity tariff he collected on behalf of the tenants in his compound in Iyana Ipaja. This can be found on page 28 of the novel.

options.a: He was out of a job and had no savings.
options.b: He needed to pay for his child's school fees
options.c: He used it for urgent medical expenses

subject: English Language

question: The following were hairstyles that Mrs Ignatius learnt in preparation for her migration abroad except
solution: Mrs Ignatius learned different Yoruba braids like Suku, Onilegogoro and Kojusoko in preparation for her family's migration to Canada. She had learnt that learning this kind of skill would fetch a lot of money in Canada that could be used to support the family. This can be found on page 20 of the novel.
subject: English Language

question: What did Bepo's fellow tenant, Iya Matthew, pour on his head after he had embezzled the compound's electricity tariff?
solution: Iya Matthew had grabbed a full bowl of elubo or cassava powder and dunked it on his head after the tenants discovered what Bepo had done. This can be found on page 28 of the novel.
subject: English Language

question: What was the topic of the debate at Bepo's send-off?

options.a: Migration: A curse or a blessing?
options.b: Who shapes the future: Teachers or parents?
options.c: The arts have contributed more to national development than the science
options.d: Public schools vs. private schools: Which is better?

subject: English Language

question: What eventually happened to Mr Ogo in the novel?
solution:
Mr Ogo was the parent at Fruitful Future School who had recommended that Bepo engage in ritual practices that would help bring more students to the school. When Bepo rejects this offer, Mr Ogo withdraws his children from the school. However, ten years later, when Bepo is watching the evening news, he discovers that Mr Ogo has been arrested for the murder of a civil servant, a woman who had consulted him to help with her infertility. During the course of the treatment, Mr Ogo had collected a total of N9 million as a fee with no tangible results. 

When the woman confronts him about this and threatens to report him to the police, he invites her to his house and murders her. This can be found on page 39 of the novel.

options.b: He relocated to the UK successfully
options.d: He opened a new private school

subject: English Language

question: According to the novel, the Gurara Waterfall was actually discovered in which year?
solution: Contrary to the European claim that it was chanced upon by some Europeans in 1925, the Gurara Waterfall was discovered by a Gwari hunter in 1745. This can be found on page 45 of the novel.
subject: English Language

question: According to the novel, who built the first-storey building in Nigeria?
solution: Henry Townsend built the first-storey building in Nigeria in 1846. It is where Ajayi Crowther stayed and made the first translation of the Yoruba Bible. This can be found on page 46 of the novel.
subject: English Language

question: What does Bepo mean by the term "new slavery"?
solution: According to Bepo, "new slavery" is when "Africans themselves, especially Nigerians, in japa fit, voluntarily and desperately [walk] into the workforce of who seemed to be the yester masters". This means that we Africans, who are migrating, are voluntarily engaging in a new kind of slavery. This can be found on page 47 of the novel.

options.a: The forced labour system in Nigerian schools
options.b: The economic challenges of the working class brought on by bad governance
options.c: The exploitation of teachers by private school owners
options.d: The voluntary migration to foreign countries to join their workforce

subject: English Language

question: What is the name of the passport office that Bepo plans to visit to renew his passport?
solution: The name of the passport office in Ibadan is the Agodi-Gate Passport Office. This can be found on page 49 of the novel.
subject: English Language

question: What was inscribed on the banner at Bepo's send-off?
solution: The banner had a large image of Bepo laughing gracefully and headlined FOR HE GAVE STARDOM HIS VERY BEST. It was subtitled "Memorable Farewell for the Most Committed Principal, Adewale Adebepo". This can be found on page 54 of the novel.

options.b: For He Gave Stardom His Very Best
options.d: We Will Miss You, Mr Bepo!

subject: English Language

question: What position did Bepo apply for when he first came to Stardom Schools?
solution: Mr Bepo first applied for the position of the school's English Teacher. He was to teach SSS1-3. This was revealed during the MD's farewell speech and can be found on pages 56-57 of the novel.
subject: English Language

question: Who did Bepo sell his car to as he planned to relocate?
solution: Mr Bepo had sold his Pathfinder Sports Utility Vehicle for N1.5 million to Stardom school's accountant, Mr Jeremi Amos. This can be found on page 60 of the novel.
subject: English Language

question: How much did Bepo give his landlord's children as he left for the airport?
solution: Bepo gave the landlord's children i.e. Jide and his sister, the sum of ₦5000 as a gift before he left for the airport.
subject: English Language

question: What course did Bepo study at the University of Benin?
solution: Bepo studied English/ History Education at the University of Benin. This can be found on page 12 of the novel.
subject: English Language

question: What subject was Sola teaching at Stardom Schools before she relocated to the UK?
solution: Sola was the Home Economics teacher at Stardom Schools before she relocated to the UK. This can be found on page 15 of the novel.
subject: English Language

question: Which historical figure, according to the novel, translated the Bible into Yoruba?
solution: According to the novel, Bishop Ajayi Crowther was the one who translated the Bible into Yoruba. This can be found on page 46 of the novel.
subject: English Language

question: What literary device is used in the expression "under the world of tears"?
solution: The phrase “under the world of tears” as used on page 8 of the novel metaphorically illustrates the depth of the principal’s emotional breakdown. It compares his sorrow to a physical state of being buried beneath tears.
subject: English Language

question: What was the mood at the beginning of the story when the principal walks to the podium?
solution: The principal's uncharacteristic silence as he walked to the podium that morning, as well as his breakdown into tears, created a tense and bewildering atmosphere for the students. There was also a feeling of uncertainty as the students had never encountered such before. The principal they have come to know and depend on was behaving quite strangely. The heavy silence, the "perplexed eyes", and the shock from both students and staff all point to a tense and uncertain atmosphere. This can be inferred from the description on page 5 of the novel.
subject: English Language

question: When Mr. Audu says, "the MD is a witch and wizard rolled into one!", what figure of speech is this?
solution: Audu’s statement is metaphorical. He doesn’t mean it literally but uses it to exaggerate the MD’s cleverness and manipulative prowess in economics. This can be inferred from the descriptions on page 5 of the novel.
subject: English Language

question: "You could see a rock carrying three other rocks on its head." What literary technique is being used?
solution: This can be found on page 7 of the novel, where Ikenna was describing the "acrobatic" Jos landscape. He says: "You could see a rock carrying three other rocks on its head. You could find a small rock confidently backing another, big enough to give birth to it." The rocks are described as if they were performing a human action—carrying—which is a personification.
subject: English Language

question: What attitude do most teachers display towards Bepo's reluctance to relocate?
solution: Many of Bepo's colleagues view his hesitation to relocate as irrational. They find it funny and think it stupid to pass on such a "golden opportunity". This can be inferred from the descriptions on pages 10 and 11.
subject: English Language

question: Why did the students expect comments from the principal after Ikenna's speech?
solution:
Mr Bepo was always known for praising such efforts. This can be inferred from the description on pages 7-8 of the novel. 

"On a day like this, he would be on top of the world. He would regard Ikenna's outing as evidence of a good education Stardom offered, and which he believed every Nigerian child deserved. The students also expected him to highlight some terms Ikenna might not have articulated so well..." (page 7-8).

options.a: It was standard procedure at Stardom Schools
options.c: The principal always appreciated students after such good speeches

subject: English Language

question: Why is Jos described as "acrobatic" in Ikenna's speech?
solution:
Rocks in Jos appear stacked in dramatic postures.

"You could see a rock carrying three other rocks on its head. You could find a small rock confidently backing another, big enough to give birth to it". (Page 7)

options.a: because of the Jos native dancers
options.b: beacause of its terrain and rock formations
options.c: because the people perform circus tricks

subject: English Language

question: What figure of speech is used in “...the microphone dropped on the floor, sending a vexatious clatter out of the twin sound boxes”?
solution: “Clatter” mimics the sound made when the microphone hits the floor. This line can be found on page 6 of the novel.
subject: English Language

question: The overall tone of the story can be described as
solution:
The story strikes a balance between a reflective sadness, stemming from Bepo's emotional turmoil, and warm character insights. Bepo grapples with the decision of whether to leave his students for a trip to the UK. His visit to the Badagry Heritage Slave Museum becomes a pivotal moment as it prompts him to reflect on the idea of "new slavery." This reflection leads him to the realisation that he is not ready to become one of these modern slaves; instead, he chooses to remain at Stardom School to nurture his students.

Throughout the narrative, a melancholic atmosphere persists.
